keyIdeas: Electrons in atoms occupy discrete energy levels (shells); electrons closest to the nucleus have the lowest energy.
Each shell (principal quantum number n = 1, 2, 3, ...) is divided into subshells labelled s, p, d (and f at higher levels); the energy order within a shell is s < p < d.

Each subshell contains atomic orbitals: s subshell has 1 orbital; p subshell has 3 orbitals; d subshell has 5 orbitals; each orbital holds a maximum of 2 electrons (with opposite spins, Pauli exclusion principle).
An s orbital is spherical; the three p orbitals are dumbbell-shaped and oriented at 90° to each other along the x, y, z axes.
Maximum electrons per subshell: s = 2, p = 6, d = 10.
Maximum electrons per shell: shell 1 = 2; shell 2 = 8; shell 3 = 18.

mustKnow.definition: A group of atomic orbitals with the same principal quantum number n; electrons in shell n = 1 are lowest in energy.
A subset of a shell with the same azimuthal quantum number; labelled s, p, or d. Within a shell, energy increases s < p < d.
A region of space that can hold up to 2 electrons with opposite spins; described by a wave function. s orbitals are spherical; p orbitals are dumbbell-shaped.
No two electrons in an atom can have the same set of quantum numbers; practically, each orbital holds at most 2 electrons with opposite spins.

markSchemePhrases: s orbital is spherical / p orbital is dumbbell-shaped
energy order s < p < d within a shell
s subshell: 1 orbital (2 e⁻); p subshell: 3 orbitals (6 e⁻); d subshell: 5 orbitals (10 e⁻)
commandWords: 'State' the shape of an s or p orbital. 'Describe' the arrangement of electrons in subshells. 'Explain' the capacity of each subshell. Questions at this level are often 'name' or 'identify' — give the exact subshell label (e.g. '2p') not just 'p orbital'.
examinerTips: Know the numbers exactly: s = 1 orbital (2e), p = 3 orbitals (6e), d = 5 orbitals (10e). These numbers are tested directly.
The shape question is almost always 'spherical' for s and 'dumbbell' (or 'figure-of-eight') for p — do not say 'circular'.
Subshells within a shell increase in energy: 2s < 2p; 3s < 3p < 3d. The 4s is anomalously lower in energy than 3d (relevant for Day 6 content).
In the context of ionisation energies, electrons in 2s are harder to remove than 2p because 2s is lower in energy.
Always pair the number with the label: '3 orbitals in the p subshell' not just 'three p's'.
workedExample:
State the number of orbitals in the 3p subshell and the maximum number of electrons it can hold.

The p subshell always contains 3 orbitals.
Each orbital holds a maximum of 2 electrons.
Maximum electrons in 3p = 3 × 2 = 6 electrons.
commonTraps: Saying an orbital 'contains' 2 electrons as a minimum — an orbital can be empty, half-filled (1e), or full (2e).
Confusing shell capacity (shell 3 holds 18 electrons) with subshell capacity (3s = 2, 3p = 6, 3d = 10: total = 18).
Drawing a p orbital as a circle rather than two lobes (dumbbell shape) — loses the shape mark.
Saying 'energy levels' when the question asks about 'subshells' — 2s and 2p are both in shell 2 but are different subshells.
Stating the 3d subshell fills before 4s — at the level of filling, 4s fills first (lower energy when filling), but 4s electrons are removed first in ionisation.
